title: Shaping Maximally Localized Wannier Functions via Discrete Adiabatic Transport
abstract: Maximally localized Wannier functions (MLWFs) are conventionally constructed by iteratively minimizing a spread functional over a high-dimensional gauge landscape. In this work, we present a non-variational constructive algorithm that unifies gauge smoothing and the eigenvalue problem of the projected position operator into a single deterministic framework. We demonstrate that discrete adiabatic transport across band degeneracies emerges naturally as an integral part of the solution procedure for the position eigenvectors. In this transport-aligned gauge, the Bloch overlaps exhibit an approximately linear phase dependence, allowing the Wannier centers to be extracted via deterministic fixed-point iterations and self-consistent updates rather than spread-functional minimization. Benchmark calculations for one- and two-dimensional systems yield spreads and orbital shapes in good agreement with standard minimization schemes. Furthermore, this analytical approach transparently isolates the physical origin of the $\mathcal{O}(L)$ mesh-dependent spread scaling ($L$ being the boundary seam resolution) observed in graphene, demonstrating that it is an intrinsic geometric manifestation of non-commuting projected position operators forcing finite gauge defects to accumulate along a one-dimensional boundary seam.
summary: The authors present a new, non-variational algorithm for constructing highly localized Wannier functions by using adiabatic transport to smooth out gauge discontinuities. This approach replaces expensive global minimization with a deterministic iterative process and provides a geometric explanation for why the spread of Wannier functions increases with grid resolution in certain 2D systems.
main_problem: The difficulty of constructing Maximally Localized Wannier Functions (MLWFs) in higher dimensions due to geometric frustration and the computational intensity of traditional variational minimization.
main_result: The development of a non-variational, deterministic algorithm that uses discrete adiabatic transport to smooth gauges, and the discovery that the O(L) scaling of spread in graphene is due to the accumulation of gauge defects along a boundary seam.
method: A constructive framework using discrete Kato parallel transport (band peeling), SVD regularization, and a 'sinc-loop' fixed-point iteration to solve for Wannier centers.
model_or_system: The method is applied to 1D and 2D isolated composite-band systems, including Krönig-Penney models, 2D square potentials, and graphene.
key_observables: Wannier function spread, Wannier centers, overlap phase planarity, and gauge defect density.
important_parameters: Mesh resolution (L), number of bands (Nc), and the projected position operator eigenvalues.
main_assumptions: The method assumes the composite energy bands are separated from the rest of the spectrum by finite gaps and that the projection operator remains C1 continuous.
figures_summary: Schematics of band peeling; cobweb diagrams of iteration convergence; flowcharts of the numerical workflow; comparisons of corrugated vs. planar overlap phases; and visualizations of MLWF orbital shapes and scaling.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of gauge frustration, proposes a transport-based algorithm, justifies it via adiabatic theory, presents a numerical workflow (sinc-loop), validates it against standard methods (Wannier90), and analyzes the geometric origin of spread scaling in graphene.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (Color online) Schematic diagram of energy band peeling across degeneracy points. In the traditional band labeling, as shown with |vn k⟩, the bands are indexed locally in k-space in ascending or- der from the bottom. On the other hand, the numbering indicated with |us k⟩provides a smoother connection. Mathematically, |us k⟩is projected to Span{|vm k+δk⟩|m = 0,1} and becomes |us k+δk⟩.
FIG. 2. Cobweb diagram for the ﬁxed-point iteration rn+1 = rn + sin[2π(x0 −rn)]/(2π) (mod 1). The dashed line denotes rn+1 = rn. For any start r0 ̸≡x0 ± 1

FIG. 3. Flowchart of the constructive numerical workﬂow used in the later results sections. The algorithm combines adiabatic peeling with ﬁxed-point iterations and self-consistent updates of the Wannier centers and projected-position matrices, followed by sequential ex- traction of localized orbitals.

FIG. 4. (Color online) Phases of the inner products of the periodic parts of BFs. nb denotes the band index. The axes are scaled by π. In panels (a) and (b), arg(⟨vk1|vk2⟩) is evaluated from the raw unprocessed periodic parts and is shown as the corrugated surface (purple in the online version), whereas arg(⟨uk1|uk2⟩) is evaluated after the transport/phase-alignment procedure and is shown as the ﬂatter surface (yellow in the online version). Accordingly, the raw overlap phase need not be planar, while the processed overlap phase becomes approximately planar; this distinction is especially visible in panel (b), where the raw surface remains corrugated whereas the processed surface is...

FIG. 5. (Color online) A graphical representation of Eq. (68). Thin curves, X0,0(r) = sin2π(x0 −r)/2π + r, are drawn with varying x0, and the thick ones are drawn with x0 obtained through the iteration of Eqs. (69) and (70). Each dot is plotted by calculating X0,0(r) = ⟨W0(r)|ˆx|W0(r)⟩with the actual WFs with varying s.
